rejectionReason: Your app is primarily a repackaged website using a web view, without sufficient native iOS functionality.
whatDeveloperDid: Submitted an app that was essentially a WKWebView loading the company's responsive website. The app had no native UI elements, no offline functionality, no push notifications, and no device-specific features. It was indistinguishable from visiting the website in Safari.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 4.2 - Design - Minimum Functionality: Your app is essentially a repackaged version of your website. We encourage you to review your app concept and incorporate native iOS features and functionality. Apps that are simply web views of a website are not appropriate for the App Store. Consider using Safari or creating a Home Screen bookmark instead.
fix: Added native iOS features: push notifications for order updates, Face ID authentication, native navigation with tab bar, offline caching of key content, Haptic feedback on interactions, widgets for quick access, and Shortcuts integration. Converted critical screens (login, checkout, profile) to native SwiftUI views.
behavioralSignals: entire app is a web view

rejectionReason: Your app provides only static information that would be better suited as a website.
whatDeveloperDid: Created a church information app that displayed static content: service times, address, staff directory, and a few pages of text about the church. No dynamic features, no community interaction, no event calendar updates, and no push notifications.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 4.2 - Design - Minimum Functionality: Your app provides limited functionality that could be accomplished with a website. The static information displayed (service times, address, staff bios) does not provide a compelling native app experience. We encourage you to add features that take advantage of the device's capabilities.
fix: Added dynamic features: live-updating event calendar with push notification reminders, sermon audio/video streaming with offline downloads, community prayer request board (with UGC moderation), in-app giving via IAP, location-based check-in, and an interactive Bible study feature with notes.

rejectionReason: Your app's user interface appears to be a web-based experience that does not follow iOS design conventions.
whatDeveloperDid: Built an app using Ionic/React Native that retained many web-like UI patterns: browser-style back buttons, web-style dropdown selects, non-native scrolling behavior, text inputs without iOS keyboard accessories, and hamburger menu instead of tab bar navigation.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 4.2 - Design - Minimum Functionality: Your app's user interface does not provide an experience that is consistent with the quality and design expectations of the App Store. The app appears to use web-based UI components that do not conform to iOS Human Interface Guidelines. Please review the iOS Human Interface Guidelines and ensure your app provides a native-quality experience.
fix: Replaced web-style components with native-looking equivalents: used bottom tab navigation instead of hamburger menu, native-style action sheets instead of web dropdowns, proper iOS navigation bar with back chevron, native scrolling with rubber-banding, and iOS-style form inputs with proper keyboard handling.

rejectionReason: Your app is a duplicate of another app and does not provide a unique experience.
whatDeveloperDid: Submitted an app that was a fork of the open-source RxMarbles app with the same binary, same functionality, and only cosmetic changes (icon color). The app was submitted from a different developer account than the original.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 4.3 - Design - Spam: Your app duplicates the content and functionality of another app already on the App Store (RxMarbles). Submitting multiple similar apps or copycat versions of popular apps is considered spam. Please ensure your app provides a unique, innovative experience.
fix: This rejection is not easily fixable since the app was fundamentally a duplicate. The developer would need to create a genuinely unique app with different functionality and value proposition. Simply changing the skin or branding of an existing app is insufficient.

rejectionReason: Your app impersonates or copies another developer's app or service.
whatDeveloperDid: After OpenAI announced Sora (AI video generation), dozens of copycat apps appeared on the App Store with names like 'Sora 2 AI Video', 'Sora Video Generator Pro', and similar variations. These apps used the Sora name and branding to mislead users, often charging subscriptions for unrelated or non-functional AI services.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 4.3 - Design - Spam: Your app appears to impersonate or misleadingly suggest an affiliation with another developer's product (Sora/OpenAI). Apps that copy the name, branding, or concept of another product to mislead users are not permitted on the App Store.
fix: These apps were removed from the App Store. Apple conducted a mass removal of Sora copycat apps. Developers of legitimate AI video tools need to use original branding that does not suggest affiliation with OpenAI or Sora.

rejectionReason: Your app's UI elements are inconsistent with iOS design patterns and appear to follow Android design conventions.
whatDeveloperDid: Ported an Android app to iOS but kept Android-style Material Design UI elements: floating action buttons (FAB), Android-style bottom sheets, Material Design text fields with floating labels, three-dot overflow menus, and Android-style toggle switches.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 4.0 - Design: Your app's user interface does not follow iOS design conventions. Several UI elements appear to be designed for another platform. Please review the iOS Human Interface Guidelines and update your app's interface to provide an experience consistent with the iOS platform.
fix: Replaced Android-style UI elements with iOS equivalents: FAB became a navigation bar '+' button, Material bottom sheets became iOS sheet presentations, floating label text fields became standard iOS text fields, three-dot menus became iOS ellipsis menus or swipe actions, and toggle switches were replaced with UISwitch.

rejectionReason: Your app uses third-party social login but does not offer Sign in with Apple as an option.
whatDeveloperDid: Implemented Google Sign-In and Facebook Login as authentication options but did not include Sign in with Apple. Since June 2020, apps that offer third-party social login must also offer Sign in with Apple as an equivalent option.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 4.8 - Design - Sign in with Apple: Your app uses third-party login services (Google, Facebook) but does not offer Sign in with Apple. Apps that use a third-party or social login service to set up or authenticate the user's primary account must also offer Sign in with Apple as an equivalent option.
fix: Added Sign in with Apple as a login option alongside Google and Facebook. Implemented the AuthenticationServices framework with ASAuthorizationAppleIDProvider. Ensured the Sign in with Apple button is displayed with equal prominence to other login options.
behavioralSignals: missing Sign in with Apple button

rejectionReason: Your Sign in with Apple button does not follow Apple's Human Interface Guidelines for button styling.
whatDeveloperDid: Implemented Sign in with Apple but used a custom-styled button that didn't match Apple's required design specifications. The button was too small, used a custom font, had rounded corners that didn't match the spec, and was placed below other login options in a smaller size.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 4.8 - Design - Sign in with Apple: Your Sign in with Apple button does not conform to the required design specifications. The button must use Apple's provided styles (ASAuthorizationAppleIDButton), be at least the same size as other login buttons, and follow the Human Interface Guidelines for Sign in with Apple button design.
fix: Replaced the custom button with Apple's native ASAuthorizationAppleIDButton, which automatically handles correct styling, sizing, and localization. Ensured the button is the same size or larger than other login buttons and is positioned with equal prominence on the login screen.
behavioralSignals: Apple login less prominent than others

rejectionReason: Your app uses Apple's copyrighted imagery without authorization.
whatDeveloperDid: Used a screenshot of the iOS home screen (SpringBoard) as a background image in the app's onboarding tutorial. The screenshot included Apple's copyrighted wallpaper, app icons, and the dock design.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 4.5.3 - Design - Apple Trademarks: Your app uses Apple's copyrighted material (iOS home screen screenshot including SpringBoard interface and Apple app icons). You may not use Apple-owned images, icons, or screenshots of Apple software in your app without explicit permission.
fix: Replaced the iOS home screen screenshot with a custom-designed illustration that conveys the same tutorial concept without using Apple's copyrighted imagery. Used generic device mockups from Apple's official marketing resources (which are permitted for certain uses) or created original artwork.
behavioralSignals: iOS home screen screenshot in app

rejectionReason: Your app provides functionality that is too similar to built-in iOS apps without providing additional unique value.
whatDeveloperDid: Created a simple calculator app and a flashlight app that replicated the exact functionality of iOS built-in apps without any additional features, customization options, or unique value proposition.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 4.2 - Design - Minimum Functionality: Your app replicates the functionality of a built-in iOS app (Calculator/Flashlight) without providing additional unique value. Simple utility apps that duplicate built-in capabilities are not appropriate for the App Store unless they offer significant additional functionality.
fix: For the calculator: added scientific calculator mode, unit conversion, calculation history, custom themes, and a widget. For the flashlight: added SOS mode, color filters, strobe patterns, screen color options, and an ambient light meter. The apps now offer meaningful functionality beyond the built-in equivalents.

rejectionReason: Your app does not provide a unique or innovative experience and lacks sufficient functionality.
whatDeveloperDid: Submitted a very simple app with a single screen that performed one basic function (e.g., a tip calculator with just a percentage slider and total display) without any additional features, customization, or polish.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 4.2 - Design - Minimum Functionality: Your app does not include sufficient content or functionality to provide a compelling App Store experience. We encourage you to review your app concept and add more features and content to provide value to users.
fix: Expanded the app with multiple features: bill splitting, custom tip presets, receipt scanning, expense history, group dining calculations, Apple Watch companion, and widgets. The app evolved from a single-function utility to a comprehensive dining expense tool.

rejectionReason: Your app's interactive elements are too small for comfortable touch interaction.
whatDeveloperDid: Designed an app with very small buttons (less than 44x44 points), tiny text (below 11pt), and densely packed interactive elements that were difficult to tap accurately on iPhone screens.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 4.0 - Design: Your app includes interactive elements that are too small for reliable touch interaction. Per Human Interface Guidelines, tappable elements should have a minimum target size of 44x44 points. Several buttons and links in your app are significantly below this minimum.
fix: Increased all tappable elements to at least 44x44 points. Increased body text to at least 13pt and added Dynamic Type support. Added proper spacing between interactive elements. Tested on the smallest supported iPhone screen size to ensure all elements are comfortably tappable.

rejectionReason: Your app's launch screen displays content in a different language than the app's primary localization.
whatDeveloperDid: Created a Japanese-language app but the LaunchScreen.storyboard contained English text ('Loading...', company name in English). The launch screen language didn't match the app's primary language, creating an inconsistent first impression.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 4.0 - Design: Your app's launch screen contains text in English, but the app's primary language is Japanese. The launch screen should be consistent with the app's primary language and localization.
fix: Localized the LaunchScreen.storyboard to display Japanese text. Best practice: use a minimal launch screen with just the app logo/icon without text to avoid localization issues entirely, matching Apple's own app launch screens.

rejectionReason: Your macOS app does not properly handle window management. Closing all windows provides no way to create a new window.
whatDeveloperDid: Built a macOS Catalyst or native macOS app that, when the user closed all windows, had no File > New Window menu item and no dock icon click handler to reopen the main window. The app was running but had no visible UI and no way to get back to it.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 4.0 - Design: Your macOS app does not properly handle window management. After closing the app's window, there is no menu option to reopen or create a new window. Users expect to be able to click the dock icon or use the File menu to access the app again.
fix: Implemented applicationShouldHandleReopen(_:hasVisibleWindows:) delegate method to show the main window when the dock icon is clicked. Added File > New Window menu item. Ensured the app activates and shows the main window when launched via Spotlight or dock.

rejectionReason: Your app's interface has significant quality issues including spelling errors, grammar mistakes, and unpolished UI elements.
whatDeveloperDid: Submitted an app with multiple spelling errors in button labels and navigation titles, grammatically incorrect alert messages, inconsistent font sizes, misaligned UI elements, and truncated text that made the app feel unfinished and unprofessional.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 4.0 - Design: Your app's user interface has quality issues that affect the user experience. We found multiple spelling errors ('Settigns', 'Purchse'), grammatically incorrect messages, truncated labels, and misaligned elements. Please polish your app's interface before resubmitting.
fix: Conducted a thorough UI review: fixed all spelling and grammar errors, ensured consistent typography, fixed all layout and alignment issues, tested on all screen sizes to prevent truncation, and had a native speaker review all user-facing text.
